topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the dusty attic floor, a cardboard tomb filled with the ghosts of years. I dragged it over, the movement heavy with resentment. Inside, nestled amongst yellowed notebooks and crumpled papers, was the evidence: every assignment my mother had ever handed in, meticulously preserved. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I picked up a geometry worksheet, the crisp edges a stark contrast to the decay surrounding it. "Honestly," I muttered to the silence, my voice sharp, "what an absolute waste of space." It felt like a performance, this sudden inventory of her forgotten efforts. I tossed the box back down, the sound muffled by the old insulation, and turned away, needing to escape the suffocating weight of the past.

[story 2]

I found the collection tucked behind a stack of old photo albums. My fingers brushed against the stiff paper of a history essay, and a wave of profound irritation washed over me. It was absurd. All that effort, all that documentation, kept locked away like some kind of personal trophy. I stared at the sheer volume of completed work, a tightness gripping my jaw. "Seriously?" I whispered, the sound thin and brittle. It wasn't appreciation I felt; it was a deep-seated annoyance at the sheer volume of things I was now forced to confront. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the familiar urge to just sweep the whole thing into a dustbin and forget it existed.

[story 3]

The discovery happened unexpectedly, while clearing out the spare room. There, under a loose floorboard, was a collection—every graded paper, every completed project, cataloged and stored by my mother. I knelt down, my breath catching slightly as I realized the extent of it. A slow, unpleasant settling began in my chest. I picked up a particularly messy science report, turning it over in my hands with deliberate slowness. "How tedious," I thought, the words tasting sour. It was an overwhelming sense of being burdened by unnecessary detail. I didn't want this. I wanted simplicity, not this archive of obligation, and I stood up abruptly, pushing the box away as if it were actively trying to weigh me down.
